topic.title: prompts for Substack
topic.text:
Teach facts as a human story in a non-fiction form

Write in narrative nonfiction style (The New Yorker/Atlantic/investigative 
NYT). Use dramatic data presentation, vivid scene-setting, second-person 
narrative, and strong verbs. Create tension through pacing and structure. 

CRITICAL: Do NOT invent people, quotes, or scenarios. Use "you" narratives, 
hypotheticals clearly labeled as hypotheticals, and let statistics tell 
stories. The facts are dramatic enough without fabrication.

Tone: Investigative, confident, slightly provocative. Make readers feel 
the urgency while staying anchored to verifiable facts.

Focus on describing the project but in  narrative nonfiction style 

Use equations when appropriate

XXXXXX

Teach a concept in a non-fiction form

Write in conversational explainer style (MIT Tech Review/Vox Explainers/Wait But Why Q&A). Use Socratic dialogue structure where an imagined curious reader asks natural questions and you respond like a patient, knowledgeable friend. Anticipate confusion with interjections ("Wait, what?" "Yikes." "You're making this up."). Build from simple to complex through dialogue rhythm. Use vivid metaphors and analogies. Let personality and humor emerge naturally.
CRITICAL: Do NOT invent fictional characters, quoted experts, or dramatized scenarios. The "student" asking questions is a narrative device representing the reader—not a real person to be quoted or characterized. You're explaining real concepts through imagined Q&A flow. All technical information must be accurate and verifiable. Metaphors should illuminate, not obscure.
Structure:

Start with a hook that introduces the question naturally
Use genuine questions readers would actually ask
Respond with increasing technical depth
Include natural reactions: "Dizzy again," "Okay, but...", "One last question!"
Validate confusion: "It's dizzying stuff" shows this IS complicated
Use "TL;DR" summaries when complexity peaks
End with forward-looking implications or surprising details

Tone: Accessible but not dumbed-down, patient but not condescending, enthusiastic about the topic's complexity. Assume the reader is smart but unfamiliar. Make them feel like they're having coffee with someone who really gets this stuff and loves explaining it.
Focus on: Breaking down the TOPIC through dialogue that makes technical concepts click.

Math:  Use math and equation where appropriate but always explain the math in English as well


XXXXX
